message.content:
Adversaries may use software packing to obfuscate their code, making it harder to detect and analyze. Software packing involves compressing or encrypting an executable, altering its file signature to evade signature-based detection. Common packers include MPRESS and UPX, but adversaries may also develop custom packers to avoid leaving recognizable artifacts. Packed executables typically decompress in memory or run within a special virtual machine. This technique is widely used by various threat actors, including APT groups like APT29, APT38, and Lazarus Group, to protect their payloads and tools. The impact of software packing includes increased difficulty in malware detection and analysis, potentially leading to prolonged undetected presence within a network.

Mitigation strategies include employing advanced behavioral analysis and heuristic-based detection methods to identify packed executables. Regularly updating antivirus and endpoint detection systems to recognize new packing techniques is also crucial. Additionally, monitoring for unusual memory activity and employing sandboxing techniques can help detect and analyze packed malware. Implementing strict code execution policies and application whitelisting can further reduce the risk of executing packed malicious code.
